title: Rajasthani Laal Maas (Simplified)

subs.name: 750 g bone-in chicken; reduce pressure-cook time to 2 whistles or stovetop cook to 20 minutes

ingredients.name: ghee
visual_checks: ghee is fundamental here; oil gives a flat result
ingredients.notes: ghee is fundamental here; oil gives a flat result

subs.name: 4 tbsp neutral oil; the curry will be less rich but still flavourful

ingredients.name: garlic, roughly crushed
visual_checks: laal maas is very garlicky — do not reduce
ingredients.notes: laal maas is very garlicky — do not reduce

ingredients.name: Kashmiri red chilli powder (for deep colour)
visual_checks: use Kashmiri chilli for colour; add 1 extra tsp hot chilli powder for more heat
ingredients.notes: use Kashmiri chilli for colour; add 1 extra tsp hot chilli powder for more heat

subs.name: use byadgi chilli powder (mild, deeply red) for colour without extreme heat, then add cayenne separately for heat

steps.text: Marinate the mutton: mix mutton pieces with curd, 1 tbsp Kashmiri chilli powder and turmeric. Cover and rest for at least 30 minutes at room temperature, or up to 4 hours refrigerated. The curd marinade tenderises the meat and helps the colour penetrate.

Heat ghee and oil together in a heavy-bottomed pot or pressure cooker on high. Add cumin, cloves, peppercorns and bay leaves; fry for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add sliced onions and cook, stirring frequently, until they are deep mahogany-brown — this takes 15 minutes and is the most important step for a dark, flavourful gravy.

Add the crushed garlic and ginger matchsticks; fry for 2 minutes. Add whole dried red chillies, the remaining red chilli powder and coriander powder. Stir and fry on medium for 1 minute — the masala should be deeply fragrant and beginning to stick to the bottom of the pot.

Add the marinated mutton (do not drain the curd marinade) and turn the flame to high. Sear the mutton pieces, turning constantly, for 5–6 minutes until the meat is no longer pink on the outside and the curd has been absorbed into the masala.

Add 1 cup hot water, season with salt, cover the pot and cook for 25–30 minutes on medium — or pressure-cook for 3 whistles on medium then allow natural release. The mutton should be very tender and fall away from the bone easily when pressed.

Uncover and check consistency. If the gravy is very thin, cook uncovered on high for 5 minutes until it reduces to a thick, oil-rimmed curry. Taste and adjust salt and heat. Finish with coriander leaves and serve with bajra roti, missi roti or steamed rice and a cooling curd raita alongside.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.

substitution_notes: mutton → 750 g bone-in chicken; reduce pressure-cook time to 2 whistles or stovetop cook to 20 minutes; ghee → 4 tbsp neutral oil; the curry will be less rich but still flavourful; red-chilli-powder → use byadgi chilli powder (mild, deeply red) for colour without extreme heat, then add cayenne separately for heat
